Not just a designer. An AI builder.

Past work · Square Banking

Building the checking account inside a point of sale.

A consumer banking experience living inside a B2B platform. An account existed. An experience didn't. This is the story of defining what activation means, designing the product that earns it, and where AI came next.

Situation · The problem
Role · Design DRITimeline · One quarterPartners · PM · Eng · Data Science · Growth
0%

of sellers who elected Checking in onboarding actually activated.

We identified a significant activation gap for new Square Banking customers. Increasing first spend was a key business objective, so understanding what prevented customers from reaching that milestone became the focus of the project.

The zero-to-one realityAn account existed. A checking experience didn't. The work wasn't polishing a mature product, it was building the product the account was supposed to be.
Situation · What Square Checking is

Your sales become spendable money, instantly.

Sellers elect Checking during Square signup. Every sale lands in the balance immediately: no waiting days for a transfer to an external bank. For a coffee shop making payroll or a barber restocking supplies, that speed is the whole point.

The platform

Square Point of Sale

Where about 80% of first sales happen. The B2B surface a seller lives in all day.

→
The moment

A sale lands

Money appears in the Checking balance the instant the card is tapped.

→
The promise

Spend it now

Debit card, deposits, payments. A consumer-grade bank for a business owner.

The B2B2C shape: Square is the platform, the seller is the consumer, and the banking experience has to feel native to both.

Situation · The before

A balance you could only look at.

1

"Add money" and "Transfer" above the fold

Two buttons, two directions, no spending. The product offered a balance and two levers, nothing that said bank.

2

The debit card buried below the fold

The strongest activation lever in the product sat in a list row with an "Activate" badge and no reason to tap it. No tracker, no momentum.

3

Money movement broken across the ecosystem

Bill Pay accepted Square Checking as a funding source, but lived as a standalone product outside Banking. Sending electronic payments or mailing checks from Banking simply didn't exist.

Scroll the phone. The navigation stays fixed, just like the real app.

The actual before. Nav fixed, everything else a scroll.
Task · What the data said

The funnel pointed to spend.

I partnered with data science on a funnel analysis of activated sellers. One behavior separated them from everyone else: early debit card engagement. Not Banking discoverability. While this was the largest driver, physical debit card and add to wallet lagged.

Engaged the debit card early
the strongest activation signal
Used deposits or bill payments
Not meaningful

Relative signal strength from the activation funnel analysis. The discoverability plays everyone wanted to build barely registered.

Task · What discovery confirmed

Sellers didn't have an awareness problem with Banking. Banking had a mental model problem with sellers.

Unaware

Card spend could happen immediately

Sellers had no idea that the moment they enrolled, a virtual card existed and could spend the same afternoon. The product never told them.

Mismatch

They expected a bank. They got a balance.

A seller's mental model of a bank: a card, deposits, ways to pay. The product offered a number and a transfer button.

Silence

The system never said what was happening

Card ordered? Printing? Shipped? Usable? Silence at every step trained sellers to stop expecting anything from the account.

Action · The call

The room wanted papercuts. The data justified a true Checking experience.

Make papercuts

Incremental fixes to the existing layout

Nudge the card module up. Tweak copy. Add an entry point or two. Low risk, low conviction, and none of it addressed the mental model gap discovery had just exposed.

The push

Rebuild the experience around the behaviors that predict activation

I made the case with my PM to revamp the surface entirely: card and money movement above the fold, system status at every step, wallet provisioning at the moment of highest motivation. Every change shipped behind an experiment with a pre-registered metric against the 32% baseline.

The experiment discipline is what made the bigger bet defensible: if the revamp didn't move the number, we'd know fast and cheap.

Action · Grounding the transition

A welcome that teaches immediate spend.

Shown exactly once, the first time a new customer lands in Banking. Its whole job is to install the right mental model in five seconds: your sales go straight to Checking, and your card can spend them right away.

1

The card is the hero

Physical and virtual, front and center. This is the object that turns a balance into a bank.

2

Categories make it concrete

Supplies, meals, inventory, subscriptions. Not "banking features" but the actual things a seller buys every week.

3

One idea, one CTA

Spend instantly after a sale. Let's go. No tour, no carousel, no cognitive tax.

EquipmentSuppliesMealsInventorySubscriptionsEquipmentSuppliesMealsInventorySubscriptions
Action · The redesign, live

A Checking experience that helps customers confidently begin banking

1

Make debit card activation the primary action

Because it represented the largest activation opportunity.

2

Surface all available spend methods

Instead of emphasizing only one, reinforcing that funds were immediately accessible.

3

Prioritize Add to Wallet

So customers could begin spending within minutes, even before receiving a physical card.

This is a working prototype: the same screens that shipped, wired up in code.

✓
Added to Apple Wallet
Action · System status

A new tracker experience gives system status at every step.

The before was silence. The after narrates the entire journey, from printing to delivered, and every state pairs the status with the single most useful action for that moment.

Processing · spend on your virtual card today
Results · Measured against the 32% baseline

Behavior changed, not just dashboards.

0%→0%
Activation for onboarding-acquired sellers, the population we set out to move. Every change shipped behind an experiment with a pre-registered metric.
0%→0%
First card spend within a month. The behavior the data said to bet on.
Held & spent
Sellers began holding balances and spending inside Square instead of sweeping payouts out. The pass-through pattern broke.

The reframe is what moved the number. Not more entry points, not louder marketing: a product that finally matched what a seller means by "bank."

Next phase · AI insights

Then the bank started thinking with you.

Activation earned the right to a bigger question: what should Banking become? I built the answer as a working prototype in Cursor, in days. Insights woven into the surface sellers already trust, and the card promoted to wallet-grade prominence.

1

The card, stacked like a wallet

The shipped version flattened cards into a list row: an engineering tradeoff. The design intent was this: cards nested with real hierarchy, the primary card in front, secondary peeking behind.

2

Insights live in the flow, not a tab

Subscription up $63. Inventory spend 12% above your three-month average. Surfaced inline, exactly where the money already lives.

The prototype: wallet-stacked cards with the hierarchy the card deserves
Next phase · Insight paired with action

Providing insight with action for spend and the greater Square ecosystem

Every insight carries its resolution. The one that matters most: payroll may not clear. The scheduled Square Payroll run exceeds the projected balance, so Banking says it out loud, before it happens, with two ways to fix it.

1

Tied into the Square ecosystem

Banking can surface signals from across Square: Payroll runs that may not clear, Invoices just paid, Trends via Appointments no-shows, Inventory cost shifts, and more. One pulse on the full business, not another dashboard.

2

Two actions, both native

Add money, or explore financing. Both resolutions live inside Square, one tap from the warning. The insight closes its own loop.

Adopted into the Banking north star, sequenced deliberately after the foundational behaviors: insights only matter to sellers whose money already lives here.

Proactive at the top of Banking, actionable in the sheet
Past work · Square Bill Pay

Tackling the friction of adding a recipient in Bill Pay.

Bill Pay adoption had plateaued. Active usage wasn't growing, and the team needed to know whether the problem was discoverability, usability, or product value before investing further. This is the story of finding the answer in a funnel, explaining it with discovery, and questioning whether the hardest step should exist at all.

Role · Lead Product DesignerGoal · Grow recurring usage and payment volume
Task · Start with the funnel, not discovery

The data showed exactly where sellers gave up.

Before any research, I started with behavioral data to identify where sellers abandoned the experience. The funnel made the answer hard to argue with.

Start payment
every seller who opened the flow
Add vendor · 54%
the cliff
Payment details · 38%
Schedule payment · 32%

Nearly half of sellers abandoned the experience while adding a recipient. No other step showed comparable friction. That is a very clear place to look.

Task · The data said where. Discovery said why.
0%

of recurring bills were managed outside Square.

Partnering with data, a bill analysis found that 80% of recurring bills were managed outside of Square, but the data alone didn't explain why. To understand the gap, I conducted discovery research to learn how sellers actually received and managed their bills.

What we foundA clear mismatch between the product and real world behavior.
Task · The insight

Sellers didn't want a better vendor form. They wanted to skip it entirely.

Improve the step

Redesign the Add Vendor form

Fewer fields, smarter defaults, inline validation. The funnel-obvious answer. And still a reconstruction job, just a slightly nicer one.

Question the step

Stop asking for information that already exists

The problem was never creating vendors. It was asking sellers to manually recreate details that already existed on the bill, in the inbox, and at the biller. So the question became: does this step need to exist at all?

This is the Bill Pay version of the Banking mental model moment: the fix isn't more surface area on a step, it's removing the step's reason to exist.

Task · Two lenses on one step

The funnel told me where to investigate. Discovery explained what to fix.

Quantitative · Behavioral data

The numbers found the step

  • 54% of sellers dropped while adding a recipient.
  • No other step had comparable abandonment.
  • This became the highest leverage opportunity in the funnel.
The questionWhy are sellers abandoning this step?
Qualitative · Seller discovery

Interviews and workflow research answered it

  • Sellers rarely memorize vendor account information.
  • Bills already contain the information needed to create a recipient.
  • Most recurring bills arrive by email or paper invoice.
  • Sellers naturally reach for the bill before they reach for Bill Pay. Behavior, not opinion.

The opportunity wasn't simplifying the form. It was eliminating the need to manually enter information sellers already had, and doing it smarter than a comparable bill pay experience from Chase or Bill.com, not a copy of one.

Action · Principles before pixels

Five principles made the roadmap obvious.

Reduce manual data entry.Every keystroke we ask for is a chance to lose the seller.

Meet sellers where bills already exist.Email, paper, portals. Go to the bill instead of making the bill come to us.

Remove unnecessary setup.Vendor records should be a byproduct of paying, not a prerequisite.

Preserve manual workflows for edge cases.Automation gets a review step and a fallback, always.

Validate each phase before investing further.Cheapest bet first, measured on the same funnel, before funding the next one.

Written down, these principles made the sequence feel inevitable: OCR first, email ingestion second, the biller network last.

Action · The before

A blank form and a reconstruction job.

1

Every bill started from zero

Search for a vendor you haven't created, type an amount, pick a date. The document holding every answer was reduced to an optional attachment.

2

The vendor form was the wall

Name, contact, address, bank account details. Exactly the information discovery showed sellers rarely had at hand.

3

54% survived it

This flow is the cliff in the funnel. Walk it yourself on the right.

Tap the pulsing spots: add a bill, then try to add the vendor.

The shipped before · manual entry, then the vendor wall
Action · Phase one, remove the entry

Scan the bill. The form fills itself.

Data showed adding vendors was the expensive step. Research showed the bill already contained the answers. So the camera became the form.

1

Scan, extract, done

Photograph or upload the bill. Recipient, amount, due date, and invoice number extract automatically.

2

The vendor creates itself

The record becomes a byproduct of paying, built from the document, with a review step before anything moves.

3

Category suggested, not demanded

Spend lands in the chart of accounts without a decision tax.

54%→62%add-vendor conversion
Phase one shipped · scan to scheduled, funded by Square Checking
Action · Phase two, meet the bill in email

Zero new UI. The inbox became the intake.

Discovery showed most recurring bills already arrived by email. So instead of asking sellers to re-enter anything, every seller got a unique Bill Pay address. Forward the bill and it lands as a draft: vendor created, details extracted by the same OCR pipeline. No screens to learn, because the work happens where the bill already lives.

1

Pure ingestion

The entire feature is an email address. The design work was restraint: the best interface for this job is no interface.

2

Compounding on phase one

The OCR investment paid twice. Same extraction, new front door.

62%→69%add-vendor conversion
Phase two shipped · a unique address, drafts appear on their own
Action · Phase three, scoped then paused

The biller network was designed. We chose not to build it yet.

The final planned phase connected Bill Pay to a national biller directory through the Paymentus API: scan a utility bill and it resolves to a recognized biller with the account number and statement balance pulled directly. The OCR foundation would prefill bill details automatically, and AI would autocategorize spend from the biller and payment history.

1

Why we paused

Two lower-effort phases had already validated the strategy and moved payment volume. The network required significantly higher engineering investment.

2

Prioritization, not loss

Validate cheap first, then ask the business to fund the expensive phase with evidence in hand. The scoped designs are ready when the data says go.

The scoped designs · a recognized biller, resolved to a real account
Results · Measured on the same add-recipient funnel

Two releases, two lifts, and volume followed.

0%→0%
Add-vendor conversion, lifted twice against the 54% baseline: 62% after OCR, 69% after email forwarding. Same funnel, same metric, every time.
+0%
Payment volume. Less typing in, more money moving out.
Recurring, up
Higher recurring bill usage. The behavior the plateau said was missing.

Adoption was never a discoverability problem. It was a friction problem, and the friction was a step that didn't need to exist.

Reflection · What this project was really about

The funnel suggested improving Add Vendor. Research suggested removing it.

That distinction shaped the roadmap. Rather than redesign a high-friction form, we invested in helping sellers use the information they already had. By validating lower cost solutions first, we increased conversion twice before asking the business to fund the most expensive phase.

This story isn't about OCR, AI, or email forwarding. Those are implementation details. Data identified the highest-friction step. Discovery explained why that step existed. The solution wasn't making the step easier. It was questioning whether it should exist at all.

Thank you.

Happy to go deeper on anything: the experiment design, the biller network scoping, the OCR pipeline, or what I'm building with Deckk.
